What I do

What I can do for you

Every care pathway begins with attentive listening and an accurate diagnosis.

Physiatric consultation

A comprehensive evaluation of musculoskeletal, orthopedic, and neurological conditions. The starting point for building a personalized care pathway.

Muscle and tendon ultrasound

A diagnostic exam performed directly in the office during the consultation. It allows real-time visualization of muscles, tendons, and joints, guides injections with precision, and shortens diagnosis times.

Ultrasound-guided injections

Ultrasound-guided injections of hyaluronic acid, PRP (platelet-rich plasma), and collagen for osteoarthritis, tendinopathies, and joint conditions. Real-time ultrasound guidance ensures millimetric precision and maximum safety.

Focal shockwave therapy

An effective treatment for chronic tendinopathies, plantar fasciitis, calcifications, and heel spurs. Non-invasive, with lasting results.

Sports medicine

Diagnosis and treatment of musculoskeletal sports injuries, for both amateur and professional athletes. Fitness-to-play evaluations for non-competitive sport.

Ozone therapy

An anti-inflammatory and analgesic treatment for spinal pain, disc herniation, and tendinopathies. Also available in systemic form (GAET).

Home physiatric visits

For patients with limited mobility, the physiatric consultation can be carried out directly at home, in Florence and the surrounding area. A complete clinical evaluation and treatment plan, with any further exams or procedures scheduled at the office if needed. Following the visit, a home physiotherapy pathway can also be set up.

Advanced procedures

Interventional physiatry

Precision ultrasound-guided procedures for the treatment of complex musculoskeletal conditions, performed directly in the office with real-time ultrasound guidance.

Lithoclasia / Barbotage for calcific deposits

An ultrasound-guided procedure for treating tendon calcifications, particularly in the shoulder's rotator cuff. Through a needle, the calcific deposit is fragmented and aspirated directly in the office, eliminating the calcium deposit responsible for the pain without resorting to surgery.

Ultrasound-guided Non-surgical Outpatient

Joint capsule hydrodistension

An ultrasound-guided treatment for adhesive capsulitis of the shoulder (frozen shoulder). A controlled volume of saline solution is injected into the capsular space under ultrasound guidance, distending the retracted capsule and progressively restoring the compromised joint mobility.

Ultrasound-guided Adhesive capsulitis Outpatient

Ultrasound-guided neurolysis

A precision procedure for treating peripheral nerve compressions — such as carpal tunnel syndrome, cubital tunnel syndrome, or entrapment neuropathies. Under ultrasound guidance, a solution is injected around the compressed nerve to free it from adhesions and reduce perineural inflammation, often achieving resolutive results without the need for surgery.

Ultrasound-guided Peripheral nerves Surgery alternative

Ultrasound-guided lumbar facet joint injection

An ultrasound-guided procedure for treating low back pain of zygapophyseal origin. Under real-time ultrasound guidance, a locally acting corticosteroid is injected near the lumbar facet joints — the small posterior joints of the spine often involved in osteoarthritis, spondylosis, and chronic low back pain. The procedure reduces local inflammation and pain in a targeted manner, with a rapid and lasting effect, without resorting to more invasive techniques.

Ultrasound-guided Chronic low back pain Outpatient
All procedures are performed under real-time ultrasound guidance, ensuring maximum precision and safety. A preliminary physiatric evaluation is required to assess the indication for treatment.

    About me

    Dr. Luca Magaton Rizzi

    I am a physician specialized in physical and rehabilitative medicine, with advanced training in musculoskeletal ultrasound — an integration that allows me to examine, diagnose, and treat within the same appointment.

    In my daily practice I aim to combine diagnostic precision with targeted, personalized treatments: from ultrasound-guided injection therapy to shockwave therapy, from ozone therapy to regenerative medicine. I have specific training in manual medicine and fascial manipulation, and pursue continuous education in interventional ultrasound.

    Alongside my clinical activity, I serve as team physician for competitive sports clubs — a context that has taught me to manage urgency, work as part of a team, and build fast, effective recovery pathways.

    I believe in medicine based on listening, on taking full charge of the person, and on pathways with a concrete goal: getting back to movement, sport, and a pain-free life.

    Education and experience

    • Degree in Medicine and Surgery, with honors, University of Trieste
    • Specialization in Physical and Rehabilitative Medicine, with honors, University of Florence
    • Erasmus+, University of Strasbourg — University Hospitals (2018–2019)
    • National SIUMB Diploma in Musculoskeletal Ultrasound
    • Advanced course in ultrasound-guided injections — University of Fribourg (CH)
    • Stecco method fascial manipulation course (levels I and II)
    • Bourdiol-Bortolin manual medicine course
    • Member of SIMFER, SITOD, affiliated with FMSI
    • Physician, ACF Fiorentina youth sector (since 2025)
    • Team physician, I Guelfi Firenze and FIDAF (since 2021)
    • Physician, Pallacanestro T Gema Montecatini (since 2025)

    Frequently asked questions

    Have questions?

    Answers to the most common questions. For anything else, I'm available via WhatsApp or email.

    The physiatrist is a physician specialized in Physical and Rehabilitative Medicine. They handle the diagnosis and treatment of musculoskeletal conditions — joint pain, tendinopathies, low back pain, sports injuries — and chronic pain management. Unlike a physiotherapist, the physiatrist is a medical doctor and can prescribe therapies, perform diagnostic procedures such as ultrasound, and carry out interventional treatments such as injections and shockwave therapy.

    Yes, I work with 12 health funds and insurance providers. To check whether yours is among those accepted, contact me directly via WhatsApp or email before booking.

    The first physiatric consultation includes an in-depth discussion of your medical history, a physical examination, and, when indicated, a musculoskeletal ultrasound performed directly in the office. At the end you'll receive a diagnosis and a personalized treatment plan.

    A session lasts about 15-20 minutes. The standard cycle involves 3-5 sessions on a weekly basis. After treatment you can resume your normal daily activities.

    Yes, I offer video consultations for preliminary assessments, second opinions, and follow-ups. Online consultations can be booked through MioDottore. Keep in mind that some procedures, such as ultrasound or interventional treatments, require an in-office visit.

    Ultrasound-guided procedures (injections, barbotage, neurolysis) are performed with thin needles and, where necessary, local anesthesia. Discomfort is generally minimal and well tolerated. Real-time ultrasound guidance reduces the number of attempts needed and increases precision, making the procedure faster and more comfortable.

    Yes, for patients with limited mobility I offer physiatric consultations directly at home, in Florence and the surrounding area. Following the visit, if indicated, a home physiotherapy pathway can also be set up. Contact me via WhatsApp or email to check availability and arrange the appointment.

    The treatment, performed under ultrasound guidance with sterile single-use materials, is generally well tolerated and anesthesia is not necessary. You may feel mild pressure or discomfort at the injection site, which resolves quickly.

    PRP is contraindicated in the presence of active infections, blood or liver disorders, low platelet count, cancer, or in patients on anticoagulant therapy. A preliminary clinical evaluation confirms suitability for treatment.

    The number of injections varies depending on the condition treated. Clinical benefit generally appears no earlier than one month after treatment and can last up to a year, with the possibility of repeating the cycle if needed.

    Response time depends on the tissue treated: tendons and ligaments, having a slower metabolism, generally take a few weeks to show noticeable improvement.

    Both methods give comparable results in treating shoulder calcifications. Barbotage (ultrasound-guided lavage and aspiration) is often preferable in acute, painful phases, while shockwave therapy is a non-invasive option when avoiding needle puncture is preferred. The choice is made together after ultrasound assessment.

    The treatment may cause transient discomfort during the session, which can be adjusted by modulating the device's intensity. Anesthesia is not necessary.

    The standard protocol generally involves a series of 3-5 weekly sessions, with the exact number determined by the site and chronicity of the tendinopathy.

    It is an ultrasound-guided procedure involving lavage and aspiration of tendon calcifications, indicated especially in calcific shoulder tendinopathy, particularly during painful phases.

    It is an ultrasound-guided interventional procedure used to treat nerve compression syndromes, such as carpal tunnel syndrome, and certain peripheral neuropathies, with the goal of releasing the nerve from adhesions or structures restricting its movement.

    Real-time ultrasound guidance allows the treatment to be performed with precision, limiting discomfort. Functional recovery is generally quick, although specific timing depends on the site and degree of nerve compression.

    The choice of medication depends on the condition, its stage (acute or chronic), and treatment goals: cortisone acts quickly on inflammation, hyaluronic acid has a viscosupplementing and protective action, and PRP stimulates tissue repair. Ultrasound assessment guides the most appropriate choice.

    There is no universal number: it depends on the medication used and the condition. Cortisone injections require a longer safety interval between sessions compared to PRP or hyaluronic acid, to avoid negative effects on tissue.
